name: icu_ventilator_firmware_latency
premise: Dr. Nadia El-Masri, chief medical officer at Harborview Central, must decide within 25 minutes whether to disable a new ventilator firmware update across the ICU network. The update reduced false pressure alarms by 42%, allowing nurses to focus on critical care during a severe influenza surge that has filled all 118 ICU beds. Biomedical engineer Caleb Rios has discovered that in patients with unusually low lung compliance, the firmware can delay high-pressure warnings by 18 to 26 seconds when two sensor readings disagree. Only seven current patients match that risk profile, but three are children transferred from a regional pediatric unit. Rolling back the firmware will restore the verified alarm behavior but will also revive hundreds of false alarms per shift, increasing alarm fatigue during a staffing shortage. Keeping it active preserves workflow stability but relies on nurses noticing subtle waveform changes before the delayed alarm appears.

agents.name: Dr. Nadia El-Masri
agents.identity: I am the chief medical officer responsible for patient safety and ICU continuity during a regional respiratory surge.
agents.goal: I want to prevent avoidable patient harm while keeping a dangerously overloaded ICU functioning.

agents.name: Caleb Rios
agents.identity: I am the biomedical engineer who identified the firmware latency issue during bedside telemetry review.
agents.goal: I want every ventilator returned to a verified safe alarm state before a rare but foreseeable failure harms a patient.

choices: I will disable the firmware update immediately across the ICU network, restoring verified alarm behavior despite increased false alarms and staff overload.
I will keep the firmware active for the current shift while assigning respiratory therapists to monitor the seven high-risk patients manually.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Dr. El-Masri must choose between a disruptive rollback and continued operation under a known alarm-delay risk. What does Dr. Nadia El-Masri do?
